description: The provided image displays a 'Check for updates' UI icon (Springer Nature badge) rather than a scientific figure from the paper. Consequently, microstructural features, diffraction patterns, or mechanical property curves related to the low-density nano-bainitic Al-C steels described in the text extraction cannot be visually analyzed. The text extraction references alloys S1-S5 with varying Al (3.92-8.08 wt%) and C (0.60-1.10 wt%) content, nano-bainitic microstructures with bainitic ferrite laths (true thickness 34-68 nm), retained austenite volume fractions (19-34%), and TiMoC or (Fe,M)xCy precipitates, but these details are not visible in the provided icon. The caption field is empty and no citation sentences were provided. Without actual figure pixels showing SEM, TEM, XRD, or mechanical test data, no scientific trends, phase distributions, or performance metrics can be extracted from the visual content.

Figure 1 comprises two panels illustrating the heat treatment protocol and kinetic analysis for low-density nano-bainitic Al-C steels. Panel (a) presents schematic temperature-time profiles for five steel grades (S1–S5). Samples were austenitized at varying temperatures (T_A): S1, S2, and S5 at 1150 °C for 15 min, S3 at 1220 °C for 20 min, and S4 at either 1150 °C or 1250 °C for 15 min. Following heating at 10 K s⁻¹, specimens were rapidly cooled at 100 K s⁻¹ to specific austempering temperatures (T_B) above the martensite start temperature (M_s): S1 and S2 to 300 °C or 350 °C, S3 to 270 °C or 350 °C, S4 to 320 °C or 350 °C (depending on T_A), and S5 to 335 °C or 365 °C. Panel (b) displays a representative dilatometric analysis showing relative length change (RLC, %) and transformation rate (s⁻¹) versus time. The bainitic transformation finish time (B_t) is determined as the point where the transformation rate decreases to a negligible level, corresponding to the onset of the plateau in the RLC curve (approximately 1500 s). This methodology enables precise determination of isothermal holding durations required for near-complete nano-bainitic transformation across the studied alloy systems.

Figure 2 illustrates the methodology for determining the retained austenite volume fraction ($V_\gamma$) in low-density nano-bainitic Al–C steels, exemplified for S1 steel (0.98 wt% C, 6.00 wt% Al) austempered at $T_B$: 350 °C. Panel (a) displays an X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern (2θ: 30–100°) showing characteristic diffraction peaks of bcc ferrite ((110)α, (200)α, (211)α, (220)α) and fcc austenite ((111)γ, (200)γ, (220)γ, (311)γ), with an inset magnifying the (200)γ peak region (yellow shaded area) indicating the integrated intensity ($I_\gamma$) used for phase quantification via the direct comparison method. Panel (b) presents MatCalc-simulated equilibrium phase fractions as a function of temperature for S1 steel, displaying the temperature-dependent stability of bcc, κ-carbide, hcp carbide, (Fe,M)xCy, and AlN phases on a logarithmic scale; the figure marks bainitic transformation temperatures at 300 °C and 350 °C, with an arrow indicating $(1-V_c) = 0.78$ at 350 °C. This combined experimental XRD and computational thermodynamic approach validates the phase quantification method, demonstrating the correlation between measured integrated peak intensities and predicted equilibrium phase fractions for the nano-bainitic microstructure.

This figure presents a combined dilatometric and optical metallographic analysis of five low-density nano-bainitic Al–C steels (S1–S5) to determine austenitizing temperatures (TA) and verify the elimination of polygonal ferrite (αp). The left column displays heating relative length change (RLC) versus temperature curves (black) with first derivatives d(RLC)/dT (red), identifying operational TA values marked by vertical dashed lines at approximately 1062 °C (S1), 1031 °C (S2), 1067 °C (S4), and ~900 °C (S5), while S3 exhibits a broadened austenitizing transition without a unique peak. The middle column shows LOM micrographs at 1050 °C (benchmark) revealing αp retention as light-etched regions in S1, S3, S4, and S5, whereas S2 appears αp-free. The right column presents LOM images at selected austenitizing temperatures (1150 °C for S1, S2, S4, S5; 1220 °C for S3), confirming complete αp elimination in S1 and S2, reduction to trace levels (≲1%) in S4 and S5, but persistent αp (~8.2% area fraction) in S3 even at 1220 °C due to its high aluminum content (8.08 wt%). These results establish 1150 °C as an optimized austenitizing temperature for S1, S2, S4, and S5 where carbide dissolution is advanced and αp is suppressed, whereas S3 requires 1220 °C yet cannot fully eliminate ferrite within practical limits, consistent with thermodynamic simulations.

MatCalc equilibrium phase-fraction-temperature diagrams for five low-density nano-bainitic Al-C steels (S1-S5), displaying calculated phase stability as a function of temperature (200–1400°C) with phase fraction on a logarithmic y-axis. Each subplot traces the equilibrium fractions of bcc ferrite (green curves), fcc austenite (red curves), κ-carbide (triangles), hcp carbide (squares), and (Fe,M)xCy MC-type carbides (circles), with S4 additionally showing TiC precipitates (blue curve). Grey boxes annotate the ferrite-free austenitizing temperature (T_A) and carbide-dissolution temperature (T_cd), with labeled values including S1 (T_A=991°C, T_cd=1103°C), S2 (T_A=929°C, T_cd=1107°C), S3 (T_A=1177°C, T_cd=1103°C), S4 (T_A=1016°C, T_cd=1134°C), and S5 (T_A=986°C, T_cd=775°C). The diagrams reveal distinct dissolution sequences: S3 exhibits the highest T_A due to elevated Al content stabilizing ferrite, while S5 shows significantly lower T_cd. These simulations guided the selection of austenitizing temperatures to achieve polygonal ferrite-free microstructures and complete carbide dissolution prior to bainitic transformation.

This figure presents cooling dilatometry curves (Relative Length Change vs. Temperature) for five low-density nano-bainitic Al–C steels (S1–S5), used to determine the martensite-start temperature (Ms) via the tangent-intersection method. Subplots (a) S1, (b) S2, (c) S3, (d) S4, and (e) S5 display the deviation from linear thermal contraction (dashed tangent lines) marking the onset of martensite formation. Determined Ms values are: S1 = 277 °C, S2 = 274 °C, S3 = 258 °C, and S5 = 331 °C. Subplot (d) for S4 specifically compares two austenitizing temperatures, showing Ms = 305 °C (1150 °C, black curve) and Ms = 310 °C (1250 °C, red curve), indicating only a marginal 5 °C increase with higher austenitizing temperature. The systematic variation in Ms correlates with alloy chemistry: S3 (highest Al, 8.08 wt%) exhibits the lowest Ms due to carbon enrichment in austenite, while S5 (lowest C and Al) shows the highest Ms. These measurements support the heat-treatment strategy where austempering temperatures were selected slightly above the measured Ms values to promote bainitic transformation while suppressing martensite formation.

Figure 6 presents bainitic transformation kinetics measured by dilatometry for five low-density nano-bainitic Al–C steels, plotting relative length change (RLC, %) versus time. Panels (a) and (b) use logarithmic time axes to compare transformation behaviors: (a) shows high-Al steels S1–S3 (Al ≥ 6 wt.%) at austempering temperatures (T_B) of 270–350 °C, while (b) shows lower-Al steels S4–S5 (Al < 6 wt.%) under varying austenitizing (T_A: 1150–1250 °C) and austempering conditions. Panels (c)–(e) replot the same data on linear time scales to facilitate reading of transformation times, with vertical dashed lines marking the approximate bainite finish time (Bt) for each condition. The curves demonstrate that S3 (8.08 wt.% Al) exhibits the slowest transformation kinetics, requiring up to 43,000 s at 270 °C, whereas S4 (Ti-microalloyed) shows accelerated nucleation due to TiMoC precipitates but suppressed progression caused by bainite sheaf impingement. S5 transforms most rapidly, completing within approximately 700–1000 s.

This chart presents the mechanical properties of five low-density nano-bainitic Al-C steels (S1-S5) under various austenitization (T_A) and austempering (T_B) conditions, displaying ultimate tensile strength (UTS, black squares), yield strength (Rp0.2, blue inverted triangles), elongation (El, red triangles), and Vickers hardness (HV30, black circles with labeled values). The x-axis identifies specific steel grades paired with heat treatment parameters: S1 and S2 at 1150°C/350°C and 1150°C/300°C; S3 at 1220°C/350°C and 1220°C/270°C; S4 at 1150°C/350°C, 1150°C/320°C, and 1250°C/350°C; and S5 at 1150°C/365°C and 1150°C/335°C. Key trends reveal that S4 achieves the highest elongation (10-12%) at T_A 1150°C/T_B 350°C while maintaining high strength (~1360 MPa), whereas S3 exhibits severely reduced ductility (2.4%) at 270°C. Lower austempering temperatures generally increase strength but decrease elongation across all alloys. Hardness values range from 443 to 500 HV30. The data demonstrate that Ti-microalloyed S4 exhibits the optimal strength-ductility balance, supporting the conclusion that careful control of alloy chemistry and heat treatment parameters is critical for maximizing the product of strength and elongation in nano-bainitic steels.

The figure presents four MatCalc-simulated Time-Temperature-Precipitation (TTP) diagrams for low-density nano-bainitic Al-C steels, showing precipitation kinetics of (Fe,M)xCy and TiMoC phases. Subplot (a) for steel S1 (representative also for S2) with austenitizing at 1150 °C and cooling at 100 K/s displays (Fe,M)xCy precipitation curves at 5%, 50%, and 95% transformation levels, indicating bainitic transformation times (Bt) of 2300 s at 350 °C and 3600 s at 300 °C. Subplot (b) for high-Al steel S3 austenitized at 1220 °C shows significantly prolonged transformation times of 14,000 s at 350 °C and 43,000 s at 270 °C due to sluggish kinetics. Subplot (c) for Ti-microalloyed steel S4 (austenitized at 1250 °C then held at 1150 °C for 900 s) uniquely depicts both (Fe,M)xCy and shaded TiMoC precipitation windows, demonstrating the suppressing effect of TiMoC on (Fe,M)xCy secondary precipitation with intermediate Bt values of 2600–3000 s at 350 °C. Subplot (d) for steel S5 austenitized at 1150 °C exhibits rapid bainitic transformation kinetics with short Bt values of 1000 s at 365 °C and 700 s at 335 °C. The figure establishes that TiMoC formation suppresses (Fe,M)xCy precipitation in S4, while moderate Al-C levels with Ni addition in S5 accelerate transformation compared to high-Al compositions.

This figure presents a comprehensive set of scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images illustrating the microstructural evolution of five low-density nano-bainitic Al–C steels (S1–S5) under various austenitizing (1150–1250 °C) and austempering (270–365 °C) conditions. The micrographs reveal nano-scale bainitic ferrite (αb) laths with true thicknesses (Lt(t)) ranging from 34 nm to 68 nm, interspersed with retained austenite (γr) in both film-like and blocky morphologies, and (Fe,M)xCy carbide precipitates located at phase boundaries. Specific subfigures highlight specimen locations (Spec.1–Spec.6) and microstructural variations: S1 and S2 display αp-free nano-bainitic structures with Vγ of 27–34%; S3 exhibits approximately 8.2% polygonal ferrite (αp) alongside bainite; S4 shows longitudinally aligned TiMoC precipitates from Ti-microalloying; and S5 contains trace allotriomorphic ferrite and lenticular martensite (M) islands at lower austempering temperatures. The images demonstrate the effects of Al content (3.92–8.08 wt%) and heat treatment parameters on bainite packet impingement, transformation completeness, and strain partitioning, supporting the optimization of strength-ductility combinations through microstructural control.

This figure presents combined SEM micrographs and EDX elemental mapping of TiMoC precipitates in steel S4 (0.87C-5.76Al-0.10Ti-0.48Mo wt%) under different heat treatment conditions. Subfigures (a), (b), and (c) display SEM images showing the morphology and distribution of TiMoC precipitates after austenitizing at 1250°C/350°C, 1150°C/350°C, and 1150°C/320°C respectively, with (b) additionally indicating an AlN particle. The longitudinal alignment of TiMoC precipitates visible in these images indicates their formation during post-casting cooling and subsequent deformation during hot rolling, with negligible secondary precipitation. Subfigure (d) presents EDX mapping of the microstructure shown in (c), displaying Fe Kα1, C Kα1,2, Ti Kα1, and Mo Lα1 distributions across a 10μm scale. The EDX maps reveal pronounced Fe depletion within the precipitates while showing strong enrichment of Ti, Mo, and C, confirming the complex carbide composition. These precipitates accelerate bainite nucleation by providing initial nucleation sites but cause impingement of bainite sheaves due to increased phase boundaries. The figure supports the conclusion that TiMoC exhibits superior thermodynamic stability compared to discrete TiC and MoC phases, effectively suppressing (Fe,M)xCy and κ-carbide formation.

Figure 11 presents a comprehensive multiscale microstructural characterization of alloy S5 (0.60C-3.92Al-0.78Ni wt%) austempered at 335 °C and 365 °C. Subfigures (a) and (b) are SEM micrographs showing nano-bainitic lamellae; at 335 °C lenticular martensite (M) embedded with bainitic lamellae is visible, while at 365 °C film-like retained austenite (γr) and impingement-free structure are observed with trace allotriomorphic ferrite (F). Subfigure (c) displays XRD patterns confirming α+γr phase constitution with no resolvable martensite, showing higher (111)γ peak intensity at 365 °C (~26% Vγ) versus 335 °C (~19% Vγ). Subfigures (d–f) are EBSD IPF maps revealing fine bainitic packets with scattered martensite at 335 °C (d, e) and slightly coarser packets with trace F at 365 °C (f). Subfigures (g–i) present KAM maps indicating local misorientation spikes near martensite at 335 °C (average KAM ~0.809° and 0.825°) versus marginally higher global KAM at 365 °C (~0.905°). The data demonstrate that lowering T_B from 365 °C to 335 °C refines bainitic laths from ~55 nm to ~41 nm, eliminates ferrite, introduces stress-assisted martensite, and increases strength (UTS: 1530 MPa vs 1315 MPa) at the expense of ductility (El: 7% vs 8.7%), supporting the conclusion that rapid bainitic transformation kinetics in S5 yield fine connected ferrite networks with temperature-dependent TRIP capability.
